exercises.title: One model for single-image, multi-image, and video
exercises.prompt: Before LLaVA-OneVision, single-image, multi-image, and video models were separate lineages. Explain LLaVA-OneVision's thesis (one curriculum, one model dominates all three via task transfer) and why a constant visual-token budget across scenarios is central.
exercises.hint: Separate specialists each won their benchmark and failed the others. OneVision: one model, one curriculum, with emergent transfer (single-image skills → video, multi-image reasoning → single-image). Constant token budget lets the same model handle all input types without blowing up context.
exercises.solution:
**The thesis:** before LLaVA-OneVision (Aug 2024) the open-VLM world had **separate lineages**: LLaVA-1.5 for single images, Mantis/VILA for multi-image, Video-LLaVA/Video-LLaMA for video. Each **won its own benchmark and failed at the others**. LLaVA-OneVision argued that **a single curriculum can train one model to dominate all three scenarios**, and — crucially — that **emergent task-transfer effects** make the unified model *beat the sum of specialists*: skills learned on single images **transfer to video**, and multi-image reasoning **transfers back to single-image** understanding. One model, trained right, is better at each task than a specialist trained only on that task.

**Why a constant visual-token budget is central:** the three scenarios have wildly different natural token counts — a single image is N patch tokens, a multi-image input is k×N, and a video is (frames)×N, which would **explode the context** if you used full tokens for each. LLaVA-OneVision **holds the total visual-token budget roughly constant across scenarios** by allocating tokens differently:
- **Single image:** spend the budget on **high resolution** (many tokens for one image, fine detail).
- **Multi-image:** **split the budget across images** (fewer tokens per image).
- **Video:** **split the budget across frames** (fewer tokens per frame, more frames).
Keeping the budget constant means **the same model and context length serve all three input types** — the model isn't overwhelmed by video's frame count or starved on single images, and the architecture doesn't change per scenario. It also makes **task transfer possible**: because the representation and budget are unified, skills learned in one scenario carry over to the others (the same token 'language' across all inputs). The constant budget is the mechanism that lets **one model genuinely be one model** across single-image, multi-image, and video — not three models in a trench coat.

exercises.title: Token budget allocation and the transfer curriculum
exercises.prompt: Implement a visual-token budget solver that holds the total constant across single-image (high-res), multi-image, and video (many frames), and describe the curriculum order (single-image → multi-image → video) that transfers skills without catastrophic forgetting. Why does curriculum order matter?
exercises.hint: Budget B fixed; single-image: B tokens at high res; multi-image: B/k per image; video: B/frames per frame (pooling). Curriculum: start single-image (build core perception), then multi-image (cross-image reasoning), then video (temporal) — each builds on the last. Order matters: foundational skills first.

exercises.title: Why unified beats specialists, and the limits of token-budget compression
exercises.prompt: Explain why a single model beats specialists at the same parameter count when curriculum is done right (task transfer, shared representations, data efficiency), the emergent capabilities reported, and the limits: when does the constant-token-budget compression hurt (video detail, long video), pointing to the video and long-context lessons.
exercises.hint: Transfer: skills compound across scenarios; shared representation is more general; more total data. Emergent: multi-camera reasoning, set-of-mark, screenshot agent. Limits: constant budget means video frames get few tokens → loses fine detail; long video exceeds even a constant budget → needs token compression/retrieval (Lessons 17-18).
exercises.solution:
**Why unified beats specialists (at equal params, with the right curriculum):**
1. **Task transfer / skill compounding:** training on all three scenarios means skills learned in one **transfer to the others** — single-image perception sharpens video frame understanding; multi-image cross-referencing improves single-image reasoning; temporal reasoning from video helps multi-image ordering. A specialist only sees its own scenario and **misses these cross-scenario gains**. The whole exceeds the sum.
2. **Shared, more general representation:** forcing one model to handle images, image sets, and video pushes it to learn a **more general visual representation** (the same token 'language' across inputs) rather than overfitting to one input shape. General representations transfer and generalize better.
3. **Data efficiency / more total data:** the unified model trains on the **union** of all three scenarios' data — far more total signal than any single specialist's dataset. More diverse data → better model (the data-centric thesis, Lesson 7).
4. **Constant token budget enables it:** because the budget is unified (Exercise 2), the model genuinely shares parameters and representation across scenarios, so transfer can happen.
Result: at the **same parameter count**, the unified OneVision model **outperforms the single-image, multi-image, and video specialists** on their own benchmarks.

**Emergent capabilities reported:** the unified training produced abilities **no specialist was explicitly trained for**:
- **Multi-camera reasoning** — reasoning across multiple simultaneous views (transfer from multi-image + video).
- **Set-of-mark prompting** — pointing/referring to marked regions and reasoning about them (transfer from grounding/multi-image).
- **iPhone-screenshot agent** — understanding UI screenshots well enough to act as a GUI agent (transfer from high-res single-image + instruction following — a direct precursor to the computer-use capstone, Lesson 25).
These **emerge** from the combination of skills, not from scenario-specific data — the hallmark of successful unification.

**The limits of constant-token-budget compression:** holding the total budget constant is powerful but has a **hard cost** for video:
- **Video frames get few tokens:** to fit many frames in a fixed budget, each frame is **pooled down to few tokens** (e.g. ~64-200), which **loses fine spatial detail per frame**. Tasks needing **fine detail within a frame** (reading text in a video, small objects, fine-grained actions) suffer — you can't both have many frames *and* high detail per frame within a fixed budget.
- **Long video breaks the budget entirely:** a fixed budget works for short clips, but a **30-minute or hour-long video** has too many frames — even at a few tokens each, you blow past any constant budget (or pool so aggressively you lose almost everything). LLaVA-OneVision's constant-budget trick **doesn't scale to long video**.
- **The fundamental tension:** **frames × tokens-per-frame = budget** — you trade temporal coverage against per-frame detail, and for long/detailed video **neither can be sacrificed enough** to fit a fixed budget.
**Where this points:** these limits motivate the next lessons:
- **Video temporal modeling (Lesson 17):** better frame sampling (dynamic-FPS, event-driven) and temporal position encoding to spend the budget *where events are*, not uniformly.
- **Long-video / million-token (Lesson 18):** when even a smart budget isn't enough, you need **token compression**, **ring/brute-force long context** (Gemini 1.5's 10M tokens), or **agentic retrieval** (VideoAgent) — fundamentally different scaling paths than a constant budget.
So LLaVA-OneVision solved 'one model for single/multi/short-video at a fixed budget' with **constant-budget reallocation + transfer curriculum** — a huge step — but its very mechanism (a fixed budget) is what **breaks for long, detail-heavy video**, handing the field the token-compression and long-context problems that Lessons 17-18 tackle. The unified model is the right default; long video needs the budget assumption relaxed.
